doc.title: Fair value option — unrealized holding gain or loss
doc.event: The company elected the fair value option for its bonds payable. At year-end the bonds' amortized carrying value and their market fair value have drifted apart — the gap is booked as an unrealized holding gain or loss, remeasuring the debt to what it would cost to settle today.

variants.label: Rates fell → the debt is worth MORE → loss

entries.caption: Year-end remeasurement: FV 480,000 vs period-4 carrying value

lines.account: Unrealized Holding Gain or Loss — Income
lines.label: FV − carrying value
lines.why: Market rates fell, so your fixed-coupon debt became MORE expensive to settle — bad news for the borrower, so a LOSS. Counterintuitive on purpose: for liabilities, fair value up = loss.
lines.trap: Students flip the sign here constantly. Think 'what would it cost me to escape this debt today?' — costlier escape = loss.

lines.label: valuation account
lines.why: The adjustment rides beside Bonds Payable so amortized cost stays intact underneath — presentation shows the bonds AT fair value.
lines.trap: Don't write Bonds Payable itself up or down — the valuation account carries the drift so the amortization schedule keeps working.

computationPaths.narration: Compare year-end fair value with the amortized carrying value off the schedule. FV 480,000 above CV → the difference is a holding LOSS in income. (One refinement Kieso notes: the piece of the change caused by the company's OWN credit risk goes to OCI, not income — flag it, don't sweat it at this level.)

variants.label: Rates rose → the debt is worth LESS → gain

entries.caption: Year-end remeasurement: FV 465,000 vs period-4 carrying value

lines.label: CV − fair value
lines.why: Fair value dropped below carrying value, so the adjustment debits — presenting the bonds at the cheaper settle-today number.
lines.trap: Same valuation account both directions; only the side flips.

lines.account: Unrealized Holding Gain or Loss — Income
lines.label: the gain
lines.why: Rates rose, your locked-in coupon looks cheap, escaping the debt got cheaper — a GAIN for the borrower.
lines.trap: Unrealized means no cash moved and nothing was retired — remeasurement only.

memorize.body: Liability mirror rule: fair value UP = LOSS, fair value DOWN = GAIN — the exact opposite of holding an asset, because you OWE this thing.
The gain/loss compares FV with the AMORTIZED carrying value (schedule number), not with face — the amortization keeps running underneath the fair value option.

questions.prompt: Under the fair value option, bonds with a period-4 carrying value have a year-end fair value of 480,000 — ABOVE carrying value. The issuer records…

distractors.feedback: The comparison base is the amortized CARRYING VALUE from the schedule, not face — amortization keeps running under the fair value option.
Sign flip: for a LIABILITY, fair value rising above carrying value is a LOSS (it costs more to settle), so the difference is FV − CV booked as a loss.

traces.label: Schedule carrying value ↔ fair value ↔ holding gain/loss

accountBank.account: Unrealized Holding Gain or Loss — Income

build.scaffold: Two lines, mirror logic: find CV on the schedule, compare with FV, and remember you're the DEBTOR — the debt getting pricier is your loss.
